title: One order_events batch through Bronze, Silver, Gold

steps.narration: 02:00. Last night's clickstream lands in the raw zone: 48,214 order_events as newline-delimited JSON, straight off the ingestion topic. It is a mess on purpose — at-least-once delivery left duplicates, a few producers dropped the device field, and the newer app build started emitting an app_version column nobody agreed to. This is what actually arrives.

state.note: raw landing zone · events/dt=2026-07-08/*.json — schema-on-read, nothing validated yet

steps.narration: Bronze = the receiving dock: accept every truck, log everything, reject nothing. The ingest job appends all 48,214 rows verbatim and adds only lineage metadata — _ingested_at, _source_file. No dedup, no casting, no dropping bad rows. Bronze's one job is to be a faithful, replayable copy of what the source sent.

state.note: append-only · +2 metadata columns · 0 rows filtered — Bronze never editorializes

steps.narration: The schema drift rides straight through, and that is correct. 2,893 rows carry no device; 3,857 rows carry an unexpected app_version. Bronze widens its schema to the union of what it sees and stores the drift as-is — because the day you need to answer “what did the source actually send on 07-08?” the answer must not have been thrown away at the dock.

state.note: Bronze schema = union of all observed columns · drift is preserved, not repaired

steps.narration: Silver = the QC station: unpack, inspect, standardize, discard the damaged — with a record. First the boring, load-bearing work: cast event_ts from string to a real timestamp, coerce event_type to the known enum, and fill the missing device with an explicit 'unknown' sentinel rather than a null that every downstream query has to remember to handle.

state.note: typing pass: event_ts → timestamp, event_type → enum, device null → 'unknown' · the drifted app_version is mapped to the agreed contract column

steps.narration: Deduplicate. At-least-once delivery meant the same event_id arrived more than once — 1,206 duplicate rows. dropDuplicates(["event_id"]) keeps one of each. This is the step that makes the pipeline idempotent: re-run the batch and Silver still holds exactly one row per real event. Bronze 48,214 → 47,008 distinct events.

state.note: dropDuplicates(["event_id"]) — dedup is a Silver responsibility, never Bronze's

steps.narration: Now the rows that fail the contract. 543 rows have a null order_id (an event with no order is uninterpretable), 208 have an unparseable event_ts, and 96 carry an event_type outside the known enum. Silver does not silently drop them — it routes all 847 to a quarantine_order_events table with a _reject_reason, so they can be inspected and backfilled once the producer is fixed.

state.note: quarantine, don't discard: 847 rows land in quarantine_order_events with a _reject_reason — a queryable dead-letter table

steps.narration: Silver's promoted table is the trustworthy one: 46,161 typed, deduped, contract-clean rows (47,008 distinct − 847 quarantined). Every column has a real type, every row satisfies the schema, and nothing was lost — the 847 rejects are one query away in quarantine. This is the table every analyst and every Gold job should build on.

state.note: Silver contract: typed · deduped · one row per event · bad rows quarantined, not vanished

steps.narration: Gold = the showroom: arranged for the customer, not the warehouse. The order_funnel_daily mart aggregates the 46,161 clean events into a handful of business rows — one per funnel stage. It joins nothing raw and exposes no nulls: the business asks “how many orders reached checkout yesterday?”, not “what's the union schema of the Bronze table?”

state.note: Gold mart order_funnel_daily: 46,161 events → 4 aggregated funnel rows, business-shaped

steps.narration: The Gold slice the fraud team actually opens: of the 6,120 orders that hit a fraud_check event and got scored, 431 landed at or above FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD and flow into the human review queue. That single number is a business fact — no drift, no quarantine, no casting to reason about. Everything ugly was handled two layers down.

state.note: Gold serves decisions: fraud_review_queue = 431 orders · the constant is applied once, here, on clean data

steps.narration: The whole journey, and the rule that governs it: each layer has a contract, and violations flow downstream, never up. Raw drift and duplicates are legal in Bronze and illegal in Gold; a business aggregate is legal in Gold and forbidden in Bronze. Put a dropDuplicates in Bronze and you have lied about the source; put a raw null order_id in a Gold mart and you have handed the ugliness to the CFO's dashboard.

state.note: Bronze: append-all, reject-nothing (48,214) → Silver: typed, deduped, quarantined (46,161 + 847 held) → Gold: joined, aggregated, decision-ready (431 in review). Contracts point one direction: down.
